title: Hypoventilation

infobox.value: [D](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q1641494 "d:Q1641494")  * **[ICD](/wiki/International_Statistical_Classification_of_Diseases_and_Related_Health_Problems "International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems")-[10](/wiki/ICD-10 "ICD-10")**: [R06.8](https://icd.who.int/browse10/2019/en#/R06.8) * **[ICD](/wiki/International_Statistical_Classification_of_Diseases_and_Related_Health_Problems "International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems")-[9-CM](/wiki/List_of_ICD-9_codes "List of ICD-9 codes")**: [786.09](http://www.icd9data.com/getICD9Code.ashx?icd9=786.09) * **[MeSH](/wiki/Medical_Subject_Headings "Medical Subject Headings")**: [D007040](https://meshb.nlm.nih.gov/record/ui?ui=D007040) * **[DiseasesDB](/wiki/Diseases_Database "Diseases Database")**: [29214](http://www.diseasesdatabase.com/ddb29214.htm)

* [Stethoscope](/wiki/Stethoscope "Stethoscope") * [Respiratory sounds](/wiki/Respiratory_sounds "Respiratory sounds")   + [Stridor](/wiki/Stridor "Stridor")   + [Wheeze](/wiki/Wheeze "Wheeze")   + [Crackles](/wiki/Crackles "Crackles")   + [Rhonchi](/wiki/Rhonchi "Rhonchi")   + [Stertor](/wiki/Stertor "Stertor")   + [Squawk](/wiki/Squawk_(sound) "Squawk (sound)")   + [Pleural friction rub](/wiki/Pleural_friction_rub "Pleural friction rub")   + [Fremitus](/wiki/Fremitus "Fremitus")   + [Bronchophony](/wiki/Bronchophony "Bronchophony")   + [Death rattle](/wiki/Death_rattle "Death rattle") * Elicited findings   + [Percussion](/wiki/Percussion_(medicine) "Percussion (medicine)")   + [Pectoriloquy](/wiki/Pectoriloquy "Pectoriloquy")   + [Whispered pectoriloquy](/wiki/Whispered_pectoriloquy "Whispered pectoriloquy")   + [Egophony](/wiki/Egophony "Egophony")

(also known as **respiratory depression**) occurs when [ventilation](/wiki/Ventilation_(physiology) "Ventilation (physiology)") is inadequate (*hypo* meaning "below") to perform needed [respiratory gas exchange](/wiki/Gas_exchange "Gas exchange").[[1]](#cite_note-1) By definition it causes an increased concentration of [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") ([hypercapnia](/wiki/Hypercapnia "Hypercapnia")) and [respiratory acidosis](/wiki/Respiratory_acidosis "Respiratory acidosis"). Hypoventilation is not synonymous with [respiratory arrest](/wiki/Respiratory_arrest "Respiratory arrest"), in which breathing ceases entirely and death occurs within minutes due to [hypoxia](/wiki/Hypoxia_(medical) "Hypoxia (medical)") and leads rapidly into complete [anoxia](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/anoxia#English "wikt:anoxia"), although both are medical emergencies. Hypoventilation can be considered a precursor to hypoxia, and its lethality is attributed to hypoxia with carbon dioxide toxicity.

Woorons X (2014). [*Hypoventilation training, push your limits!*](http://www.hypoventilation-training.com/Activity.html). Arpeh. p. 164. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-2-9546040-1-5](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-2-9546040-1-5 "Special:BookSources/978-2-9546040-1-5").

Weingarten, Toby N; Deljou, Atousa; Sprung, Juraj (14 January 2025). ["Research and clinical implications of emerging evidence regarding patterns of postoperative opioid-induced respiratory depression"](https://www.bjbms.org/ojs/index.php/bjbms/article/view/11123). *Biomolecules and Biomedicine*. **25** (2): 327–337. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.17305/bb.2024.11123](https://doi.org/10.17305%2Fbb.2024.11123). [PMC](/wiki/PMC_(identifier) "PMC (identifier)") [11734825](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC11734825).

Zubieta-Calleja GR, Paulev PE, Zubieta-Calleja L, Zubieta-Calleja N, Zubieta-Castillo G (September 2006). "Hypoventilation in chronic mountain sickness: a mechanism to preserve energy". *Journal of Physiology and Pharmacology*. **57** (Suppl 4): 425–430. [PMID](/wiki/PMID_(identifier) "PMID (identifier)") [17072073](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17072073).

Dy, Sydney M.; Gupta, Arjun; Waldfogel, Julie M.; Sharma, Ritu; Zhang, Allen; Feliciano, Josephine L.; Sedhom, Ramy; Day, Jeff; Gersten, Rebecca A. (2020-11-19). [Interventions for Breathlessness in Patients With Advanced Cancer](https://effectivehealthcare.ahrq.gov/products/dyspnea-advanced-cancer/report) (Report). Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality (AHRQ). [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.23970/ahrqepccer232](https://doi.org/10.23970%2Fahrqepccer232).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Not to be confused with [Hyperventilation](/wiki/Hyperventilation "Hyperventilation") or [Hyperventilation syndrome](/wiki/Hyperventilation_syndrome "Hyperventilation syndrome").

Insufficient breathing

Medical condition

| Hypoventilation | |
| --- | --- |
| Other names | Respiratory depression |

**Hypoventilation** (also known as **respiratory depression**) occurs when [ventilation](/wiki/Ventilation_(physiology) "Ventilation (physiology)") is inadequate (*hypo* meaning "below") to perform needed [respiratory gas exchange](/wiki/Gas_exchange "Gas exchange").[[1]](#cite_note-1) By definition it causes an increased concentration of [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") ([hypercapnia](/wiki/Hypercapnia "Hypercapnia")) and [respiratory acidosis](/wiki/Respiratory_acidosis "Respiratory acidosis"). Hypoventilation is not synonymous with [respiratory arrest](/wiki/Respiratory_arrest "Respiratory arrest"), in which breathing ceases entirely and death occurs within minutes due to [hypoxia](/wiki/Hypoxia_(medical) "Hypoxia (medical)") and leads rapidly into complete [anoxia](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/anoxia#English "wikt:anoxia"), although both are medical emergencies. Hypoventilation can be considered a precursor to hypoxia, and its lethality is attributed to hypoxia with carbon dioxide toxicity.

## Causes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hypoventilation&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Causes")]

Hypoventilation may be caused by:

* A medical condition such as [stroke](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke") affecting the [brainstem](/wiki/Brainstem "Brainstem")
* Voluntary breath-holding or underbreathing, for example, [hypoventilation training](/wiki/Hypoventilation_training "Hypoventilation training")[[2]](#cite_note-2) or the [Buteyko method](/wiki/Buteyko_method "Buteyko method").
* [Medication](/wiki/Medication "Medication") or drugs, typically when taken in accidental or intentional [overdose](/wiki/Overdose "Overdose"). Opioids are a well-known cause of drug-induced hypoventilation due to their depressant effects on the central respiratory centers. Recent studies have highlighted patterns of postoperative opioid-induced respiratory depression that may help identify at-risk patients.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
* [Hypocapnia](/wiki/Hypocapnia "Hypocapnia"), which stimulates hypoventilation
* [Obesity](/wiki/Obesity "Obesity"); see [Obesity hypoventilation syndrome](/wiki/Obesity_hypoventilation_syndrome "Obesity hypoventilation syndrome")

* [Chronic mountain sickness](/wiki/Chronic_mountain_sickness "Chronic mountain sickness"), a mechanism to conserve energy.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

* [Paralyzing](/wiki/Paralysis "Paralysis") [venom](/wiki/Venom "Venom"), such as that of the [blue ringed octopus](/wiki/Blue-ringed_octopus "Blue-ringed octopus").

### Medications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hypoventilation&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Medications")]

As a [side effect](/wiki/Adverse_drug_reaction "Adverse drug reaction") of medicines or [recreational drugs](/wiki/Recreational_drug "Recreational drug"), hypoventilation may become potentially life-threatening. Many different [central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system") (CNS) [depressant](/wiki/Depressant "Depressant") drugs such as [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol"), [benzodiazepines](/wiki/Benzodiazepines "Benzodiazepines"), [barbiturates](/wiki/Barbiturates "Barbiturates"), [GHB](/wiki/Gamma-Hydroxybutyric_acid "Gamma-Hydroxybutyric acid"), [sedatives](/wiki/Sedatives "Sedatives"), and [opioids](/wiki/Opioid "Opioid") produce respiratory depression when taken in large or excessive doses, or mixed with other depressants. Strong opiates (namely [fentanyl](/wiki/Fentanyl "Fentanyl"), [heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin"), and [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine")), [barbiturates](/wiki/Barbiturates "Barbiturates"), and certain benzodiazepines (such as [alprazolam](/wiki/Alprazolam "Alprazolam")) are known for depressing respiration. In an overdose, an individual may cease breathing entirely (go into [respiratory arrest](/wiki/Respiratory_arrest "Respiratory arrest")) which is rapidly fatal without treatment. Opioids, in overdose or combined with other depressants, are notorious for such fatalities. Nevertheless, appropriate use of opioids in the right setting, as seen in patients with advanced cancer or severe chronic non-malignant pain, have been shown to be helpful, when used under appropriate medical care and guidance.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Treatment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hypoventilation&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Treatment")]

Respiratory stimulants such as [nikethamide](/wiki/Nikethamide "Nikethamide") were traditionally used to counteract respiratory depression from CNS depressant overdose, but offered limited effectiveness. A new respiratory stimulant drug called [BIMU8](/wiki/BIMU8 "BIMU8") is being investigated which seems to be significantly more effective and may be useful for counteracting the respiratory depression produced by opiates and similar drugs without offsetting their therapeutic effects.

If the respiratory depression occurs from [opioid overdose](/wiki/Opioid_overdose "Opioid overdose"), usually an opioid antagonist, most likely [naloxone](/wiki/Naloxone "Naloxone"), will be administered. This will rapidly reverse the respiratory depression unless complicated by other depressants. However an opioid antagonist may also precipitate an [opioid withdrawal syndrome](/wiki/Opioid_addiction_and_dependence "Opioid addiction and dependence") in chronic users. [Mechanical ventilation](/wiki/Mechanical_ventilation "Mechanical ventilation") may still be necessary during initial resuscitation.

## Associated conditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hypoventilation&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Associated conditions")]

Disorders like [congenital central hypoventilation syndrome](/wiki/Congenital_central_hypoventilation_syndrome "Congenital central hypoventilation syndrome") (CCHS) and [ROHHAD](/wiki/ROHHAD "ROHHAD") (rapid-onset obesity, hypothalamic dysfunction, hypoventilation, with autonomic dysregulation) are recognized as conditions that are associated with hypoventilation. CCHS may be a significant factor in some cases of [sudden infant death syndrome](/wiki/Sudden_infant_death_syndrome "Sudden infant death syndrome") (SIDS), often termed "cot death" or "crib death".

The opposite condition is [hyperventilation](/wiki/Hyperventilation "Hyperventilation") (too much ventilation), resulting in low carbon dioxide levels ([hypocapnia](/wiki/Hypocapnia "Hypocapnia")), rather than hypercapnia.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hypoventilation&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: See also")]
